explanation: The property expresses that IF the system is currently in PCV mode (PCVMode is true), and ventilation is active (ventilating is true), AND the user has selected PSV mode (PSVModeSelected is true), THEN the transition to PSV mode (PSVMode) should occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory phase (inspiratoryPhaseEnd). To precisely capture 'the transition...shall occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory time if the change mode command has been received from the GUI', we use the implication: globally, when we are in PCV mode, ventilating, and have received the command to transition to PSV, then at the next occurrence of inspiratoryPhaseEnd, PSVMode is active. Previous naive attempts might have missed the synchronization between the mode selection and the end of inspiratory phase.
output_LTL: G( (PCVMode & ventilating & PSVModeSelected) -> F( inspiratoryPhaseEnd & X(PSVMode) ) )

explanation: The natural language specifies that when the ventilator is in PCV mode (PCVMode), and the ventilation is on (ventilating), a transition to PSV mode (PSVMode) is allowed—but only under the additional condition: at the end of the PCV inspiratory time (inspiratoryPhaseEnd) and if the user has given the change mode command via the GUI (PSVModeSelected). Thus, the LTL formula globally enforces: if the system is in PCV mode, ventilation is ongoing, the inspiratory phase just ended, and a PSVMode selection command has been issued, then in the next step the system should switch to PSV mode. Previous LTL properties may not fully capture (a) the dependency on four conditions and (b) the precise placement of the mode shift (only right when inspiratory phase ends), as well as (c) requiring the 'ventilation is on' constraint.

The input requires that (1) while ventilation is on, a transition from PCV mode to PSV mode may occur only if the mode change command has been received from the GUI, and (2) this transition can only occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory phase (i.e., at 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd'). Given the available atomic propositions, we interpret 'ventilation is on' as 'ventilating', 'PCV mode active' as 'PCVMode', 'PSV mode active/selected' as 'PSVMode', and 'change mode command has been received' as 'PSVModeSelected'. We want to specify that: whenever the system is ventilating and in PCVMode, and at the end of the inspiratory phase (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), if PSVModeSelected (change command) is true, then at the next step, PSVMode becomes true (system transitions to PSV); otherwise, it remains in PCVMode. Thus, the formula encodes that the transition is strictly conditioned both by the timing (end of PCV inspiratory phase) and the receipt of the command while ventilation is ongoing. Previous LTL formulas that might simply specify a response to the command at any time, or the transition at phase end alone, would not restrict the conditions to only when both are satisfied.

The requirement specifies that in PCV Mode, a transition to PSV Mode should occur (i) only if ventilation is on, and (ii) at the end of the PCV inspiratory time, and (iii) if the 'change mode' command has been received from the GUI (indicated by PSVModeSelected). From the provided atomic propositions, 'ventilating' maps to requirement (i), 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd' can proxy the termination of the inspiratory phase (end of PCV inspiratory time) for (ii), and 'PSVModeSelected' maps to (iii). The LTL property encodes that globally, whenever we are in PCVMode and ventilation is on, if at the end of the inspiratory phase the change mode to PSV was selected, then eventually the system must enter PSVMode. This captures the conditional, event-driven transition described. Previous LTLs may have failed to require all three conditions (PCV mode, ventilation, end of PcV inspiration, and command received) before permitting or requiring the transition, or failed to ensure a 'response' in terms of eventually entering PSVMode.

The property specifies that, whenever the system is in PCV mode and ventilation is on, if (at the end of an inspiratory phase) a command to switch to PSV mode has been received, then a transition to PSV mode should occur. Here, 'PCVMode' is true when pressure controlled ventilation is active, 'ventilating' when the ventilation is on, 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd' signals the end of inspiration, and 'PSVModeSelected' indicates a change mode command to PSV from the GUI. The transition itself is assumed to mean that the system enters PSV mode (i.e., 'PSVMode' becomes true). We express this as: globally, whenever we are in PCVMode and ventilating, if at the end of inspiratory phase and the change-mode command to PSV has been received, then in the next step, PSV mode must be active. This captures the requirement from the natural language. Previous LTL formulas may have not captured the simultaneity of 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd' and 'PSVModeSelected' as the trigger for transition, or the fact that this transition condition is only relevant while ventilation is on and PCV mode is active.

The input natural language describes two conditions for a transition from PCV mode to PSV mode: (1) ventilation is on, and (2) at the end of a PCV inspiratory time (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), if the mode change command has been received from the GUI (PSVModeSelected). To capture both, we need to express that globally, whenever we're in PCVMode and 'ventilating' is true, then at the next 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd', if PSVModeSelected occurs, then PSVMode will hold. This LTL formula ensures that whenever in PCV mode and ventilation is active, if the PSVModeSelected is true at the end of the inspiratory phase, then PSV mode should be active at the next step. This directly encodes the causal and temporal constraints described, while ignoring unrelated transitions or edge cases not specified (e.g., not specifying what happens if ventilation is off or no mode change command is received).

The given natural language requires that, if the ventilator is on and a change mode command to switch to PSV (Pressure Support Ventilation) is received from the GUI, then the mode transition from PCV (Pressure Controlled Ventilation) to PSV should occur only at the end of the PCV inspiratory time (that is, at the end of the current PCV inspiration). The atomic propositions available are 'ventilating', 'PSVModeSelected', and 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd'. The translation therefore states: always, if the ventilator is running AND the command to select PSV has been received, THEN at the END of inspiration phase in PCV mode (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), the PSV mode is actually entered. Since we do not have an explicit atomic proposition for the actual transition, we interpret entering PSV Mode as 'PSVMode'. The LTL property requires that whenever ventilation is on and PSVModeSelected is true, if inspiratoryPhaseEnd occurs, then PSVMode must eventually become true (and it cannot occur before that phase end).

The natural language describes a mode transition condition: the system should transition from PCV to PSV if ventilation is on, and this transition must occur at the end of the PCV inspiratory time ONLY IF the change mode command (to PSV) has been received from the GUI. We encode that globally, if we are in PCV mode, ventilation is on, and at the end of the inspiratory phase ("inspiratoryPhaseEnd"), and user has initiated a PSV mode switch ("PSVModeSelected"), then in the next step the PSV mode must be active ("PSVMode"). This LTL property captures the conditional and synchronized nature of the transition AND requires that the mode transition can ONLY occur at the end of the inspiratory time (i.e., at "inspiratoryPhaseEnd"). Previous properties might fail if they do not synchronize the mode transition with both the ventilation status and the exact end of inspiration, or if they are too permissive/trigger mode change at other times.
output_LTL: G(PCVMode & ventilating & inspiratoryPhaseEnd & PSVModeSelected -> X PSVMode)

explanation: The requirement states that if ventilation is active (ventilating), and a change mode command to switch to PSV has been received from the GUI (PSVModeSelected), the switch from PCV to PSV mode must occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory time (inspiratoryPhaseEnd while in PCVMode). In LTL terms, whenever we are ventilating, in PCV mode, and the switch to PSV mode is requested, then, at the end of the current inspiratory phase, PSV mode should be activated. This is written as: Globally, if ventilating & PCVMode & PSVModeSelected & inspiratoryPhaseEnd, then PSVMode should follow immediately (in the next state, using the X operator). This translation captures the essential time ordering and logical preconditions described by the natural language. Previous LTL properties might neglect that the transition must occur specifically at the end of the inspiratory phase of PCV, or they might misplace the trigger (e.g., not accounting for the mode change request or the active ventilation condition), or misplace the temporal relation (not requiring the next state to be PSVMode). This LTL is faithful to the provided requirement and the available atomic propositions.

The input natural language states that when in PCV mode (Pressure Controlled Ventilation mode) and ventilation is on, a transition to PSV mode (Pressure Support Ventilation) should only occur at the end of a PCV inspiratory time, provided that a change mode command (to PSV) has been received from the GUI. This means that a mode switch is allowed only under three conditions: (1) the system is currently ventilating, (2) the system is in PCVMode, and (3) PSVModeSelected (the command to switch to PSV) has been triggered. The actual transition should align with inspiratoryPhaseEnd (the end of a PCV inspiration). 

The LTL formula:
G((PCVMode & ventilating & PSVModeSelected & inspiratoryPhaseEnd) -> X PSVMode)

This translates as: Always, if all of the following occur at the current moment—PCVMode, ventilating, PSVModeSelected, and inspiratoryPhaseEnd—then in the next step the system should be in PSVMode. 

Alternative or previously considered forms such as:
- Requiring only the occurrence of PSVModeSelected at any point, or responding to PSVModeSelected without synchronizing with the end of inspiratoryPhase, would not enforce the temporal synchronization required by the specification.
- Allowing transition at arbitrary points (not just at the end of PCV inspiration) would violate the requirement that it must be 'at the end of PCV inspiratory time.'

Thus, this LTL precisely encodes the 'guarded transition' described by the user.
output_LTL: G((PCVMode & ventilating & PSVModeSelected & inspiratoryPhaseEnd) -> X PSVMode)
